metadata.title: Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Control (CMC) Information for Human Gene Therapy Investigational New Drug Applications (INDs)
metadata.issuing_authority: FDA Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research (CBER)

rules.rule_text: IND sponsors must provide sufficient CMC information to assure product safety, identity, quality, purity, and strength (including potency) of the investigational gene therapy product.
rules.verbatim_quote: The purpose of this guidance is to inform sponsors how to provide sufficient CMC information required to assure product safety, identity, quality, purity, and strength (including potency) of the investigational product (21 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 312.23(a)(7)(i)).

rules.rule_text: FDA may place the IND on clinical hold if the IND does not contain sufficient CMC information to assess risks to subjects in the proposed studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA may place the IND on clinical hold if the IND does not contain sufficient CMC information to assess the risks to subjects in the proposed studies (21 CFR 312.42(b)(1)(iv)).

rules.rule_text: Manufacturing changes that could affect product safety, identity, quality, purity, potency, or stability must be submitted as an information amendment to the IND prior to implementation.
rules.verbatim_quote: Thus, if a manufacturing change could affect product safety, identity, quality, purity, potency, or stability, you should submit the manufacturing change for FDA review prior to implementation.
rules.exceptions: Minor changes may be reported in annual reports per 21 CFR 312.31(a)(1)

rules.rule_text: Commercial IND submissions and Master Files must be submitted in eCTD format beginning May 5, 2018. BLAs must be submitted in eCTD.
rules.verbatim_quote: (BLAs) must be submitted in eCTD, and commercial IND submissions and Master Files must be submitted in eCTD beginning May 5, 2018 (Ref. 3).
rules.exceptions: Investigator-sponsored and expanded access INDs (emergency use INDs, treatment INDs) are excluded from the eCTD requirement.

rules.rule_text: IND must contain a copy of all labels and labeling to be provided to each investigator in the clinical study.
rules.verbatim_quote: Your IND must contain a copy of all labels and labeling to be provided to each investigator in the clinical study (21 CFR 312.23(a)(7)(iv)(d)).

rules.rule_text: IND products must bear a label with the statement 'Caution: New Drug—Limited by Federal (or United States) law to investigational use'.
rules.verbatim_quote: IND products must bear a label with the statement, "Caution: New Drug—Limited by Federal (or United States) law to investigational use" (21 CFR 312.6).
rules.exceptions: Products derived from autologous donors meeting 21 CFR 1271.90(a) may have different labeling requirements

rules.rule_text: Autologous cell products must be labeled 'FOR AUTOLOGOUS USE ONLY' and 'NOT EVALUATED FOR INFECTIOUS SUBSTANCES' if donor testing and screening is not performed.
rules.verbatim_quote: For example, for cells intended for autologous use, you must label the product "FOR AUTOLOGOUS USE ONLY" (21 CFR 1271.90(c)(1)) and "NOT EVALUATED FOR INFECTIOUS SUBSTANCES" if donor testing and screening is not performed (21 CFR 1271.90(c)(2)).

rules.rule_text: IND must contain either an environmental analysis or a claim for categorical exclusion.
rules.verbatim_quote: Your IND must contain either an environmental analysis or a claim for categorical exclusion (21 CFR 312.23(a)(7)(iv)(e)).
rules.exceptions: Most INDs are eligible for categorical exclusion under 21 CFR 25.31(e)

rules.rule_text: IND must contain a description of the drug substance and drug product, including physical, chemical, or biological characteristics, manufacturing controls, and testing information to ensure acceptable limits for identity, strength (potency), quality, and purity.
rules.source_section: IV.B. Drug Substance and Drug Product
rules.verbatim_quote: Your IND must contain a description of the DS (21 CFR 312.23(a)(7)(iv)(a)) and DP (21 CFR 312.23(a)(7)(iv)(b)), including the physical, chemical, or biological characteristics, manufacturing controls, and testing information, to ensure the DS and DP meet acceptable limits for identity, strength (potency), quality, and purity.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide the name and address of each manufacturer, including contract manufacturer(s), involved in the manufacture, testing, and storage of the drug substance.
rules.verbatim_quote: You must provide the name and address of each manufacturer, including contract manufacturer(s), involved in the manufacture, testing, and storage of the DS (21 CFR 312.23(a)(7)(iv)(a)).

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide a list of all materials used in manufacturing and a description of the quality or grade of these materials.
rules.verbatim_quote: You must provide a list of all materials used in manufacturing and a description of the quality or grade of these materials (21 CFR 312.23(a)(7)(iv)(b)).

rules.rule_text: For allogeneic cells or tissues, sponsors must perform donor screening and testing as required in 21 CFR Part 1271, Subpart C. Donors must be screened for HIV, HBV, HCV, human TSE (including CJD and vCJD), and Treponema pallidum (syphilis).
rules.verbatim_quote: For allogeneic cells or tissues, you must perform donor screening and testing, as required in 21 CFR Part 1271, Subpart C... Donors of all types of cells and tissues must be screened for risk factors and clinical evidence of relevant communicable disease agents and diseases, including: human immunodeficiency virus (HIV); hepatitis B virus (HBV); hepatitis C virus (HCV); human TSE, including Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD) and variant CJD (vCJD); and Treponema pallidum (syphilis) (21 CFR 1271.75).
rules.exceptions: Autologous cells do not require donor eligibility determination per 21 CFR 1271.90(a)(1)

rules.rule_text: Donor eligibility testing must use appropriate FDA-licensed, approved, or cleared donor screening tests and must be performed by a CLIA-certified laboratory.
rules.verbatim_quote: For donor eligibility testing, you must use appropriate FDA-licensed, approved, or cleared donor screening tests (21 CFR 1271.80(c)). Moreover, the required testing must be performed by a laboratory that either is certified to perform such testing on human specimens under the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments of 1988 (42 U.S.C. 263a) and 42 CFR Part 493 or has met equivalent requirements as determined by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (21 CFR 1271.80(c)).

rules.rule_text: Master cell bank (MCB) qualification for viral vector production must include tests to ensure absence of microbial contamination (sterility, mycoplasma, adventitious viral agents), species-specific pathogens, and confirm cell identity. Retroviral contamination testing using reverse transcriptase assays and transmission electron microscopic analysis is recommended.
rules.verbatim_quote: Cell bank qualification should include tests to: Ensure absence of microbial contamination, including sterility, mycoplasma (and spiroplasma for insect cells), and adventitious viral agents. For cell lines used for production of viral vectors, we recommend that you test for retroviral contamination, using reverse transcriptase assays and transmission electron microscopic analysis.
rules.exceptions: WCB qualification is less extensive than MCB

rules.rule_text: For bacterial cell banks used to manufacture a DNA plasmid, testing must include: bacterial host strain identity, plasmid presence, bacterial cell count, bacterial host strain purity (negative for bacteriophage), plasmid identity by RE analysis, and full plasmid sequencing.
rules.source_section: V.A.2.c.iii.c. Bacterial or Microbial Master Cell Banks
rules.verbatim_quote: For bacterial cell banks used to manufacture a DNA plasmid, we recommend the testing include: Bacterial host strain identity; Plasmid presence, confirmed by bacterial growth on selective medium, restriction digest, or DNA sequencing; Bacterial cell count; Bacterial host strain purity (no inappropriate organisms, negative for bacteriophage); Plasmid identity by RE analysis; Full plasmid sequencing.
rules.exceptions: MCBs may not be necessary for all manufacturing situations if plasmid intermediate is appropriately qualified

rules.rule_text: For viral vectors ≤40 kb, sponsors must fully sequence the vector, analyze the sequence, submit an annotated sequence of the entire vector, and evaluate the significance of all discrepancies between expected and determined sequences.
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that you fully sequence all vectors that are 40 kb or smaller, analyze the sequence, and submit an annotated sequence of the entire vector. You should provide an evaluation of the significance of all discrepancies between the expected sequence and the experimentally determined sequence and an evaluation of the significance of any unexpected sequence elements, including open reading frames.
rules.exceptions: For vectors >40 kb, summarize extent and results of sequence analysis; sequence the gene insert, flanking regions, and modified regions

rules.rule_text: For non-replicating gene therapy viral vectors, sponsors must test for replication-competent virus at multiple points during production. For adenoviral-based products, sponsors must qualify the MVB for RCA and test each production lot DS or DP, with a maximum level of 1 RCA in 3×10^10 viral particles.
rules.verbatim_quote: Most adenoviral-based products used for gene therapy applications are designed to be replication defective... for adenoviral-based gene therapy products, we recommend that you qualify your MVB for RCA and test either the DS or DP of each production lot for RCA. We recommend a maximum level of 1 RCA in 3×10^10 viral particles.

rules.rule_text: For retroviral-based products, sponsors must perform RCR testing at multiple points during production of a retroviral vector to ensure the absence of replication-competent retrovirus.
rules.verbatim_quote: To ensure the absence of RCR, you should perform testing for RCR at multiple points during production of a retroviral vector.
cross_references: FDA Guidance: Testing of Retroviral Vector-Based Human Gene Therapy Products for RCR During Product Manufacture and Patient Follow-up

rules.rule_text: Drug substance specifications must be listed in the original IND submission, with acceptance criteria established and justified for identity, strength (potency), quality, and purity. Phase 1 IND must include specifications with established acceptance criteria for safety testing.
rules.verbatim_quote: You should list DS specifications in your original IND submission... the testing plan submitted in your IND should be adequate to describe the physical, chemical, or biological characteristics of the DS necessary to ensure that the DS meets acceptable limits for identity, strength (potency), quality, and purity (21 CFR 312.23(a)(7)(iv)(a)). Your IND should include specifications with established acceptance criteria for safety testing at Phase 1.
rules.exceptions: Acceptance criteria may be adjusted throughout development stages

rules.rule_text: Residual host cell DNA from continuous non-tumorigenic cell substrates must be limited to less than 10 ng/dose and the DNA size must be below approximately 200 base pairs.
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that you limit the amount of residual DNA for continuous non-tumorigenic cells to less than 10 ng/dose and the DNA size to below approximately 200 base pairs (Ref. 12).
rules.exceptions: For tumor-derived cells (e.g., HeLa, HEK293T), additional controls on specific residual DNA quantities may be needed; limit of specific residual DNA quantities may vary

rules.rule_text: For parenteral drugs administered other than intrathecally or intraocularly, the endotoxin acceptance criterion must not exceed 5 EU/kg body weight/hour. For intrathecally-administered drugs, the limit must be 0.2 EU/kg body weight/hour. For intraocularly-administered drugs, the limit must be no more than 2.0 EU/dose/eye.
rules.verbatim_quote: For any parenteral drug, except those administered intrathecally or intraocularly, we recommend that the upper limit of acceptance criterion for endotoxin be 5 Endotoxin Unit (EU)/kg body weight/hour. For intrathecally-administered drugs, we recommend an upper limit of acceptance be set at 0.2 EU/kg body weight/hour... we recommend acceptance criterion of not more than (NMT) 2.0 EU/dose/eye for injected or implanted DPs or NMT 0.5 EU/mL for ophthalmic irrigation products.
rules.exceptions: Different limits apply for intraocular ophthalmic irrigation products (NMT 0.5 EU/mL)

rules.rule_text: For ex vivo genetically modified cells, a minimum acceptable viability of at least 70 percent is recommended for product release.
rules.verbatim_quote: For ex vivo genetically modified cells, we recommend a minimum acceptable viability of at least 70 percent. If this level cannot be achieved, we recommend that you submit data in support of a lower viability specification, demonstrating, for example, that dead cells and cell debris do not affect the safe administration of the product and/or the therapeutic effect.
rules.exceptions: Lower viability may be acceptable with supporting data demonstrating dead cells/debris do not affect safety or therapeutic effect

rules.rule_text: For ex vivo genetically modified cells administered immediately after manufacturing, an in-process sterility test on a sample taken 48 to 72 hours prior to final harvest is recommended for product release.
rules.source_section: V.B.3.c. Description of Manufacturing Process and Process Controls (3.2.P.3.3)
rules.verbatim_quote: For ex vivo genetically modified cells that are administered immediately after manufacturing, we recommend a negative test result from an in-process sterility test (on a sample taken 48 to 72 hours prior to final harvest) for release of the DP.
rules.exceptions: Standard compendial sterility tests (USP <71>) may be used for products with adequate shelf life

rules.rule_text: In all cases where product release is prior to obtaining sterility test results, if a sterility test is determined positive after product administration, the sterility failure must be reported to both the clinical investigator and FDA. Investigation results and corrective actions must be included in an information amendment submitted within 30 calendar days.
rules.verbatim_quote: You should report the sterility failure to both the clinical investigator and FDA... We recommend that you include results of the investigation of the cause of the sterility failure and any corrective actions in an information amendment submitted to your IND within 30 calendar days after initial receipt of the positive culture test result (21 CFR 312.31).

rules.rule_text: If a patient experiences a serious and unexpected adverse drug event attributable to a non-sterile gene therapy product, this information must be reported to FDA in an IND safety report no more than 15 calendar days after initial receipt of the information.
rules.verbatim_quote: If the patient experiences any serious and unexpected adverse drug event that could be from administration of the non-sterile gene therapy product, then you must report this information to FDA in an IND safety report no more than 15 calendar days after your initial receipt of the information (21 CFR 312.32(c)).

rules.rule_text: Identity assays must uniquely identify a product and distinguish it from other products in the same facility. Identity testing is performed on the final labeled product to verify its contents.
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that identity assays uniquely identify a product and distinguish it from other products in the same facility. This test is performed on the final labeled product to verify its contents (21 CFR 610.14).

rules.rule_text: Drug product lots that fail to meet specifications must not be used in the clinical investigation without FDA approval.
rules.verbatim_quote: Product lots that fail to meet specifications should not be used in your clinical investigation without FDA approval.

rules.rule_text: For viral vectors, safety testing must include tests for mycoplasma or adventitious viruses performed on cell culture harvest material (cells and supernatant) prior to further processing, to maximize sensitivity.
rules.verbatim_quote: To maximize the sensitivity of safety testing, it is important that you perform each test at the stage of production at which contamination is most likely to be detected. For example, tests for mycoplasma or adventitious viruses (in vivo or in vitro) should be performed on cell culture harvest material (cells and supernatant) prior to further processing, e.g., prior to clarification, filtration, purification, and inactivation.

rules.rule_text: Dose-determining assays (e.g., vector genome titer by qPCR, transducing units, plaque-forming units, flow cytometry for transduced cells) must be qualified as suitable for use prior to initiating clinical studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: To ensure consistent dosing in your clinical investigations, assays used to determine dose (e.g., vector genome titer by quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR), transducing units, plaque-forming units, flow cytometry for transduced cells) should be qualified as suitable for use prior to initiating clinical studies.

rules.rule_text: LOA (Letter of Authorization) is required for all information submitted by another individual in the IND. The LOA must include a description of the cross-referenced information and identify where it is located.
rules.verbatim_quote: Proper authorization may be granted with a Letter of Authorization (LOA) from the individual who submitted the information (21 CFR 312.23(b)). We recommend that the LOA include a description of the information being cross-referenced (e.g., reagent, container, vector manufacturing process) and identify where that information is located (e.g., file name, reference number, volume, page number).

rules.rule_text: Bovine materials used in manufacturing require documentation of source, location where herd was born, raised, and slaughtered, and information relevant to TSE risk. Sample COAs and COOs for all bovine material lots used in cell and virus bank establishment must be provided, consistent with 9 CFR 113.53.
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that you provide information on any bovine material used in manufacturing, including the source of the material; information on the location where the herd was born, raised, and slaughtered; and any other information relevant to the risk of transmissible spongiform encephalopathy (TSE)... You should provide sample COAs and COOs (in section 3.2.A.2 of the CTD) for all bovine material lots used in the manufacture and establishment of cell and virus banks consistent with 9 CFR 113.53.

rules.rule_text: A Quality Unit must be in place with responsibilities including implementing procedures to prevent microbial contamination, cross-contamination, and product mix-ups, and procedures to investigate lot failures and out-of-specification results.
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, we recommend that INDs at all stages of development should have established written SOPs to ensure proper manufacturing control and oversight. Manufacturing oversight is usually performed by a dedicated Quality Unit, the duties of which include implementing procedures to prevent microbial contamination, cross-contamination, and product mix-ups. Your Quality Unit should have procedures in place to investigate lot failures, out-of-specification results, and ways to implement corrective actions.

rules.rule_text: An Adventitious Agents Safety Evaluation section (3.2.A.2) is required in the IND, providing information on viral safety studies and the risk of potential contamination with adventitious agents.
rules.verbatim_quote: You should provide information assessing the risk of potential contamination with adventitious agents. For non-viral adventitious agents, we recommend that you provide detailed information on the avoidance and control of transmissible spongiform encephalopathy agents, bacteria, mycoplasma, and fungi... For viral adventitious agents, we recommend that you provide information on viral safety studies.

rules.rule_text: For genetically modified cell-based gene therapy products, the drug product specifications must include an acceptance criterion for the minimum number of genetically modified cells in a product lot. The product dose should be based on the total number of genetically modified cells.
rules.verbatim_quote: If your final product is genetically modified cell-based gene therapy, you should have an acceptance criterion for the minimum number of genetically modified cells in a product lot. We recommend that the product dose for such products be based on the total number of genetically modified cells.

rules.rule_text: For ex vivo genetically modified cells, identity testing must include an assay to measure the presence of vector (expression assay or restriction digest) or genetic change AND an assay specific for the cellular composition of the final product (e.g., cell surface markers).
rules.verbatim_quote: If the final product is ex vivo genetically modified cells, we recommend that identity testing include an assay to measure the presence of vector (i.e., expression assay, restriction digest) or genetic change and an assay specific for the cellular composition of the final product (e.g., cell surface markers).

rules.rule_text: For a licensure-supporting BLA, assay validation must be complete before submission. All analytical procedures must have a validation plan in place prior to initiating later phase clinical studies, and analytical procedures used to determine dose must be validated prior to initiating clinical studies to support licensure.
rules.verbatim_quote: For all analytical procedures, we recommend that you evaluate assay performance throughout product development, have a validation plan in place prior to initiating later phase clinical studies, and complete validation before BLA submission... you should validate tests used to determine dose prior to initiating clinical studies to support licensure.
rules.exceptions: Validation not required for original Phase 1 IND submissions; qualification of safety and dose-determining assays required pre-Phase 1
